CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (Intro → theory on chaos → method/analysis → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contribution early (eqs. (1)-(2) and the CFN claim). Notation is generally consistent and definitions are given, but a missing symbol for the Hadamard product in the intro and a few minor notational ambiguities reduce clarity. Figures and tables are repeatedly referenced and described in the text (e.g. Figs. 1–4, Tables 1–3) and appear informative, though the provided excerpt contains duplicated sentences and formatting/OCR glitches (repeated paragraphs and messy citation fragments) that slightly impede reading. Overall the writing is clear and precise apart from these presentation issues. | B(4): The paper is well-organized (Intro → dynamics/lemmas → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (definition (1)–(2), claim of non-chaotic dynamics and empirical comparison). Notation for the model and gates is consistent and the theoretical parts (Lemmas 1–2 and proofs) are clearly presented. Presentation suffers from copying/formatting glitches in the provided text (repeated paragraphs, mangled table lines, missing figures/tables in the excerpt) and a few minor typos/wording issues (e.g. "usefull", awkward phrasing in places), which merit tidy-up but do not obscure the paper’s arguments.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (Intro → theory on chaos → method/analysis → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contribution early (eqs. (1)-(2) and the CFN claim). Notation is generally consistent and definitions are given, but a missing symbol for the Hadamard product in the intro and a few minor notational ambiguities reduce clarity. Figures and tables are repeatedly referenced and described in the text (e.g. Figs. 1–4, Tables 1–3) and appear informative, though the provided excerpt contains duplicated sentences and formatting/OCR glitches (repeated paragraphs and messy citation fragments) that slightly impede reading. Overall the writing is clear and precise apart from these presentation issues.

Notation for the model and gates is consistent and the theoretical parts (Lemmas 1–2 and proofs) are clearly presented. Presentation suffers from copying/formatting glitches in the provided text (repeated paragraphs, mangled table lines, missing figures/tables in the excerpt) and a few minor typos/wording issues (e.g. "usefull", awkward phrasing in places), which merit tidy-up but do not obscure the paper’s arguments.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a clearly specified new gated RNN architecture (Eq. (1)–(2), the CFN) together with a theoretical dynamical-systems analysis (Lemma 1 and Lemma 2) proving the model is ‘‘chaos‑free’’ (only the zero attractor) and contrasting this with chaotic behavior of LSTM/GRU (Section 2 and synthetic/trained examples). While word-level language modeling is a well-studied task, the combination of a minimalist gating design that is provably non‑chaotic and empirical parity with LSTMs is a notable and well-differentiated contribution (architecture + dynamical analysis + experiments). The technical novelty is moderate (a simple architectural variant rather than a large methodological leap), but the rigorous dynamical perspective and proofs make the contribution informative to experts. | B(4): The paper proposes a simple gated RNN variant (eqs. (1)–(2)) and provides a formal dynamical analysis (Lemma 1 and Lemma 2, eq. (10)) proving the induced system is never chaotic, contrasting this with demonstrations that LSTM/GRU can exhibit strange attractors (sec. 2.1, eqs. (7)–(9)). It clearly differentiates its contribution from prior work by combining a compact architectural change with provable, qualitative dynamical properties and empirical results showing comparable word-level language modeling performance (sec. 3). This is a notable and non-trivial perspective (theoretical guarantee + empirical parity) rather than a purely incremental tweak, and would be of clear interest to experts.

While word-level language modeling is a well-studied task, the combination of a minimalist gating design that is provably non‑chaotic and empirical parity with LSTMs is a notable and well-differentiated contribution (architecture + dynamical analysis + experiments). The technical novelty is moderate (a simple architectural variant rather than a large methodological leap), but the rigorous dynamical perspective and proofs make the contribution informative to experts.

(10)) proving the induced system is never chaotic, contrasting this with demonstrations that LSTM/GRU can exhibit strange attractors (sec. 2.1, eqs. (7)–(9)). It clearly differentiates its contribution from prior work by combining a compact architectural change with provable, qualitative dynamical properties and empirical results showing comparable word-level language modeling performance (sec. 3). This is a notable and non-trivial perspective (theoretical guarantee + empirical parity) rather than a purely incremental tweak, and would be of clear interest to experts.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s principal theoretical claims are well supported: Lemma 1’s bound on activation/decay follows correctly from |tanh(x)|≤|x| and iteration, and Lemma 2 gives a valid contraction argument (|u_t(i)| ≤ σ(C)|u_{t−1}(i)| with σ(C)<1) showing all autonomous CFN trajectories tend to zero. The demonstration that LSTM/GRU can exhibit chaotic behavior is plausibly supported by a synthetic two-unit example and sensitivity experiments on a trained LSTM, although no formal Lyapunov analysis is provided. Experimental comparisons to LSTMs use matched parameter counts and similar training protocols, but the methodology has minor weaknesses (no random seeds/multiple runs or statistical tests reported, and some hyperparameters like dropout (p,q) differ between models), so empirical claims are convincing but not exhaustively validated. | B(4): The paper’s main theoretical claims are clearly stated and the mathematical arguments for the CFN’s non‑chaotic behavior are sound: Lemma 1’s recurrence bound follows directly from |tanh(x)|≤|x| and Lemma 2 correctly shows contraction |u_t(i)| ≤ σ(C)|u_{t-1}(i)| so all autonomous trajectories converge to 0. The multilayer decay estimate (11) is plausible though only sketched rather than fully derived. The experimental comparison to LSTMs/GRUs on Penn Treebank and Text8 supports the empirical claim but the methodology omits statistical tests and uses an unusual normalized‑gradient optimizer and limited tuning details, so fairness/reproducibility concerns remain (possible minor weakness that does not invalidate the main conclusions).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s principal theoretical claims are well supported: Lemma 1’s bound on activation/decay follows correctly from |tanh(x)|≤|x| and iteration, and Lemma 2 gives a valid contraction argument (|u_t(i)| ≤ σ(C)|u_{t−1}(i)| with σ(C)<1) showing all autonomous CFN trajectories tend to zero. The demonstration that LSTM/GRU can exhibit chaotic behavior is plausibly supported by a synthetic two-unit example and sensitivity experiments on a trained LSTM, although no formal Lyapunov analysis is provided. Experimental comparisons to LSTMs use matched parameter counts and similar training protocols, but the methodology has minor weaknesses (no random seeds/multiple runs or statistical tests reported, and some hyperparameters like dropout (p,q) differ between models), so empirical claims are convincing but not exhaustively validated.

The multilayer decay estimate (11) is plausible though only sketched rather than fully derived. The experimental comparison to LSTMs/GRUs on Penn Treebank and Text8 supports the empirical claim but the methodology omits statistical tests and uses an unusual normalized‑gradient optimizer and limited tuning details, so fairness/reproducibility concerns remain (possible minor weakness that does not invalidate the main conclusions).
